narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection from law school stings, especially when you've set your sights on becoming a lawyer, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to passing the bar exam as an uphill battle—about a 79% chance of not making it. Picture your journey like a story the model pieced together from key chapters of your background, starting with your academic standing in law school itself, which turned out to be the biggest signal. Your Year 3 decile of 2nd place from the bottom stood out as the strongest pull toward struggle, suggesting that by the end of your program, you were finding the material tougher than most of your peers. That was reinforced by your Year 1 decile of 6th, which also pointed in the same direction—not a disastrous start, but enough to hint at mounting challenges ahead. Layered on top was your upper-middle family income, which the model saw as less of a safety net compared to others who might have more resources to lean on during intense prep. Your LSAT score of 35 and undergrad GPA of 2.8 added to this narrative, painting a picture of solid but not standout preparation coming into law school. Even factors like being a white male studying full-time wove in as subtle threads that, in combination, tipped the scales toward predicting you'd face real hurdles on the bar. It's not that any one piece defined you, but together they told the model a story of someone who might need extra support to defy the odds. This isn't the end of your story—you've got target_law drive that shines through—and many have turned similar profiles around with focused effort. We're rooting for you to rewrite this chapter elsewhere. Keep pushing forward.
